description: This figure presents engineering stress-strain curves of two austenitic high-Mn low-density steels under different annealing conditions, tested at room temperature with an initial strain rate of 5×10⁻⁴ s⁻¹. The two steels are 2Al steel (Fe–30.5Mn–2.1Al–1.2C, wt.%) and 8Al steel (Fe–30.5Mn–8.0Al–1.2C, wt.%). Four curves are included: black curve for 2Al annealed at 450°C for 1h, red curve for 2Al annealed at 650°C for 72h, green curve for 8Al annealed at 450°C for 1h, blue curve for 8Al annealed at 600°C for 24h. The 8Al-600°C/24h sample shows the highest ultimate tensile strength ~1180 MPa with elongation ~37%, while 2Al-450°C/1h exhibits the highest elongation ~75% with ultimate tensile strength ~935 MPa. Long-time annealing at 650°C for 2Al reduces elongation to ~35% due to grain boundary M₃C carbide precipitation, while 8Al-450°C/1h achieves ~65% elongation and ~895 MPa strength. The figure demonstrates that Al content and annealing-regulated precipitations significantly modify the mechanical performance of the steels, and proper annealing of high-Al steels enables a good combination of high strength and acceptable ductility.

This figure plots the strain hardening rate (unit: GPa) as a function of true stress (unit: MPa) for 2Al steels (nominal composition Fe–30.5Mn–2.1Al–1.2C wt.%) under tensile deformation at an initial strain rate of 5×10⁻⁴ s⁻¹. Two samples with different annealing conditions are compared: the black curve corresponds to 2Al steel annealed at 450 °C for 1 h, while the red curve corresponds to 2Al steel annealed at 650 °C for 72 h. The strain hardening behavior is divided into two stages by a dashed boundary at ~1100 MPa true stress. In the first hardening stage, both samples exhibit a rapid decrease in strain hardening rate from ~5 GPa to ~2 GPa as true stress increases. In the second hardening stage, the 450 °C/1 h annealed sample maintains a relatively stable strain hardening rate of ~1.9 GPa up to high true stress, while the 650 °C/72 h annealed sample fractures at ~1150 MPa true stress without entering the stable second hardening stage. This result supports the conclusion that intergranular M3C-type carbides in the long-annealed 2Al steel interact with deformation twins, which are supposed to contribute to the second hardening stage, to trigger stress concentration and premature intergranular fracture, leading to limited uniform elongation.

This set of micrographs characterizes the deformation structure and fracture features of long-annealed 2Al steel (Fe-30.5Mn-2.1Al-1.2C, annealed at 650℃ for 72 h). Subfigure (a) is an ECCI image with 500 nm scale bar, showing dislocation cell blocks in the sample strained to 0.2 true strain. Subfigure (b) with 10 μm scale bar presents the distribution of intergranular incoherent M₃C-type carbides along grain boundaries. Subfigure (c) is an ECCI image with 1 μm scale bar, revealing the interactions between deformation twins and intergranular M₃C-type carbides in the fractured sample. Subfigure (d) with 10 μm scale bar displays the fracture surface, confirming an intergranular fracture mode. The results demonstrate that impingement of deformation twin tips on coarse intergranular M₃C carbides induces local shear stress concentration at carbide interfaces, triggering intergranular fracture instead of additional work hardening, leading to a limited uniform elongation of ~35% for the long-annealed 2Al steel.

This figure plots the strain hardening rate as a function of true stress for annealed 8Al steels (nominal composition Fe–30.5Mn–8.0Al–1.2C wt.%) subjected to tensile deformation at an initial strain rate of 5×10⁻⁴ s⁻¹. Two curves are included: the black curve corresponds to the sample annealed at 450 °C for 1 h, while the red curve corresponds to the sample annealed at 600 °C for 24 h. The x-axis is true stress with a range of 400 to 1800 MPa, and the y-axis is strain hardening rate ranging from 0 to 5 GPa. Both samples show an extremely high initial strain hardening rate (~5 GPa), which drops sharply to ~2 GPa around 1100 MPa where the two curves intersect. After the intersection point, the strain hardening rate of the 450°C/1h sample decreases continuously until fracture at ~0.9 GPa, while the 600°C/24h sample first increases to a peak of ~2.2 GPa before decreasing, with a higher fracture true stress. This indicates that longer higher-temperature annealing regulates κ-carbide precipitation to modify the strain hardening behavior and improve the strength of the 8Al austenitic steel, supporting the conclusion that annealing treatment can tune the mechanical performance of high-Mn low-density steels via precipitation state control.

This figure displays the deformation microstructures of annealed 8Al (Fe-30.5Mn-8.0Al-1.2C) austenitic steels. Subfigure (a), a 500 nm-scale electron channeling contrast imaging (ECCI) micrograph, shows Taylor lattice structures in the short-annealed (450℃, 1 h) 8Al steel after tensile deformation to 0.3 true strain. Subfigure (b) is a 20 nm-scale dark-field transmission electron microscopy (DF-TEM) image presenting the distribution of nano-sized κ-carbides in the short-annealed sample, with a selected area electron diffraction pattern inset. Subfigure (c), a 500 nm-scale ECCI micrograph, reveals planar dislocation arrays (marked by arrows) in the long-annealed (600℃, 24 h) 8Al steel deformed to 0.15 true strain. Subfigure (d) is a 20 nm-scale DF-TEM image showing cuboidal κ-carbides in the long-annealed sample, with arrows indicating sheared carbide particles and a diffraction pattern inset. It confirms that shearable nano κ-carbides promote planar slip and planar dislocation substructure formation, contributing to the excellent combination of 990 MPa yield strength, 1180 MPa ultimate tensile strength and 37% uniform elongation of long-annealed 8Al steel.
